candidate_check.description: The Ubuntu operating system must generate audit records for successful/unsuccessful uses of the usermod command.

expected.substring: -a always,exit -S all -F path=/usr/sbin/usermod -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=privileged-usermod

check_content_excerpt:
Verify that an audit event is generated for any successful/unsuccessful use of the "usermod" command.
 
Check the currently configured audit rules with the following command:
 
$ sudo auditctl -l | grep -w usermod
 
-a always,exit -S all -F path=/usr/sbin/usermod -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=privileged-usermod
  
If the command does not return a line that matches the example or the line is commented out, this is a finding.
  
Note: The "key=" value is arbitrary and can be different from the example output above.

expected_values.substring: -a always,exit -S all -F path=/usr/sbin/usermod -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=privileged-usermod

fix_text_excerpt:
Configure the audit system to generate an audit event for any successful/unsuccessful uses of the "usermod" command.
 
Add or update the following rules in the "/etc/audit/rules.d/stig.rules" file:
 
-a always,exit -F path=/usr/sbin/usermod -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k privileged-usermod
    
To reload the rules file, issue the following command:
  
$ sudo augenrules --load
 
Note: The "-k <keyname>" at the end of the line gives the rule a unique meaning to help during an audit investigation. The <keyname> does not need to match the example above.
title: The Ubuntu operating system must generate audit records for successful/unsuccessful uses of the usermod command.
